title: Why the body keeps protesting even after the mind says it is fine
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
Your brain says you're fine. Your body says otherwise.

I know this experience. I've lived it. You wake up, you tell yourself the logical facts. The threat is gone. You are safe now. You have resources. You have support. Your mind nods along, agreeing. Everything is fine. But your body doesn't get the memo. Your shoulders stay tight. Your stomach clenches. Your heart races when a door slams. You feel exhausted for no reason. You get sick constantly. You can't sleep through the night.

Here's the thing. This disconnect between what your mind knows and what your body feels isn't a character flaw. It's not a sign that you're broken. It's not something you're doing wrong. It's a survival system working exactly as designed. The problem is that the design was never meant to handle what you've been through.

Bessel van der Kolk spent decades studying this. He wrote a book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). His research shows that trauma literally reshapes the body's response systems. The brain's alarm center, the amygdala, becomes hypervigilant. The stress hormone system gets stuck in overdrive. The body learns to prepare for danger even when the mind knows danger has passed. It's not a choice. It's biology.

Think about it. Your nervous system evolved over millions of years to detect threats. It doesn't care about logic. It doesn't care about your therapist's reassuring words. It doesn't care that you've done the work. It cares about survival. And survival is a full-body job.

The nervous system doesn't take orders from your thoughts

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, explains this beautifully. Your nervous system has its own intelligence. It scans your environment constantly, looking for cues of safety or danger. This happens below conscious awareness. You can't think your way into feeling safe. You can't reason your way out of a trauma response. The body is running its own program.

I remember sitting in my living room, logically knowing I was safe. My front door was locked. No one was coming for me. I had a stable job. I had people who loved me. And yet my hands were shaking. My jaw was clenched. My chest felt like someone was sitting on it. I kept telling myself "I'm fine, I'm fine, I'm fine." But my body was screaming otherwise. Right?

Here's something wild. Your body can hold a trauma response for decades without you knowing it. You might think you've "dealt with" something because you can talk about it without crying. But talking about it uses the left hemisphere - language, logic, narrative. The trauma might still be living in your right hemisphere, in your body, in your implicit memory.

Pat Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy addresses exactly this gap. She noticed that clients could have years of talk therapy and still be triggered by the same things. Because the body hadn't been included in the conversation. The body was still bracing, still guarding, still waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Try this. Stand up for a moment if you can. Feel your feet on the ground. Now push gently against a wall with both hands. Feel the resistance. Feel your own strength pushing back. That's your body remembering that it can take action. That it's not helpless. That it has agency. Sometimes the body needs to complete a movement it was never allowed to make. That's not weird. That's healing.

That's because the body's alarm system has no language center. It doesn't process words. It processes sensations, vibrations, tones of voice, facial expressions, smells, sounds. When you say "I'm fine" but your body remembers being trapped, being hurt, being helpless, it doesn't believe you. It believes what it remembers. And what it remembers is stored in tissue, in fascia, in the nervous system itself.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, calls this "the trauma vortex." The body holds incomplete survival responses. When you were threatened, your body prepared to fight, flee, or freeze. But if you couldn't complete that response, the energy got stuck. It stays in the body. Years later, a trigger can activate that same stuck energy. Your body goes right back to that moment of threat, even if your mind is fully in the present.

So you might find yourself shaking after a conversation that wasn't even conflictual. You might feel panicked in a crowded grocery store for no reason. You might have nightmares about events you barely remember consciously. The body keeps protesting because it never finished protesting the first time.

Why traditional talk therapy often misses the mark

This is where so many people get stuck. They go to therapy. They talk about their childhood. They understand their patterns. They gain insight. And they still can't sleep through the night. They still have panic attacks. They still dissociate during sex. They still feel numb or overwhelmed without warning.

Look, talking is important. I'm not saying it isn't. But talking only reaches the neocortex, the thinking brain. The trauma lives in the limbic system and the brainstem. It lives in the body. You can't talk your way out of a body-based problem. You need to work with the body directly.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She builds on Porges' work and gives concrete practices for calming the nervous system. Not through thinking, but through sensation, through movement, through breathing, through connection. These aren't woo-woo fixes. They're physiological interventions.

I've found that when I slow down and actually notice what my body is doing, something shifts. I'm not trying to change it. I'm not trying to fix it. I'm just paying attention. And somehow, that attention itself begins to regulate the system. Wild, right? The body doesn't need to be told what to do. It needs to be witnessed.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get enough attention. The way chronic stress changes your relationship with pain. When your nervous system has been in overdrive for years, your pain threshold shifts. Things that shouldn't hurt, hurt. Things that should hurt, don't register at all. Your body's volume knob is broken.

This is what the research on central sensitization shows. The spinal cord and brain start amplifying pain signals. Fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, irritable bowel - these aren't imaginary. They're the body's honest response to a nervous system that never got to complete its stress cycle. The energy that was meant for fight or flight got trapped. And trapped energy has to go somewhere.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've been told your pain isn't real, if you've been dismissed by doctors, if you've started to doubt your own body - I want you to know something. Your body isn't lying. It's speaking the only language it has. The question isn't whether the pain is real. The question is what your body is trying to tell you through it.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium plays a role in nervous system regulation. It helps with muscle tension, sleep, and stress response. I'm not saying supplements fix trauma. They don't. But they can support the body's ability to regulate while you do the deeper work.

The protest is a message, not a malfunction

Here's what I want you to really hear. Your body's protest isn't your enemy. It's not a betrayal. It's not a sign that you're broken beyond repair. It's a communication. Your body is telling you something. It's saying "I'm still carrying this. I haven't been able to let it go. I need help."

When you fight your body's responses, you add another layer of stress. You tense against the tension. You panic about the panic. You shame yourself for shaking. This creates a loop that keeps the nervous system stuck in threat mode. The protest gets louder because it's not being heard.

What if instead, you could say to your body "I hear you. I see you. I know you're scared. I know you're tired. I'm here with you." Not to make it go away. Just to be present. Just to let the body know it's not alone anymore. That's a radical shift. And it's the beginning of real change.

Janina Fisher, who works with trauma and dissociation, talks about this as "befriending the body." She says the body isn't trying to ruin your life. It's trying to survive. And the strategies that kept you alive in the past may no longer be needed, but the body doesn't know that yet. It needs to be shown, not told.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure stimulation can help activate the parasympathetic nervous system. It's not a cure. But it can help the body feel held, contained, and safe enough to begin letting go.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

Patience is not passive. It's active endurance.

I won't lie to you. This work takes time. The body doesn't heal on a timeline. It doesn't care about your productivity goals or your desire to just be done with this already. The body heals in its own rhythm. And that rhythm can be frustratingly slow.

Sit with that. How does it feel to hear that? Maybe there's relief. Maybe there's anger. Maybe there's despair. All of those are valid. All of them belong in this process. The body keeps protesting, and part of the healing is learning to tolerate that protest without trying to shut it down.

You're not doing anything wrong when your body still reacts. You're not failing. You're not broken. You're a human being with a nervous system that learned to survive in an unlivable situation. That survival instinct doesn't just switch off because you've done some healing. It takes repeated, consistent, gentle experiences of safety to retrain the system.

Dan Siegel talks about "integration" as the key to healing. Integration means different parts of the system learning to work together. The mind and the body. The thinking brain and the survival brain. The past and the present. When these parts are integrated, the body doesn't have to keep protesting because the system knows it's safe now. But integration takes time. It takes practice. It takes grace.

So if your body is still protesting today, I see you. I'm in the same place. Some days are easier than others. Some days the protest is loud and I just have to ride it out. Some days I can feel the calm settling in. Both are okay. Both are part of this messy, nonlinear, deeply human process of healing.

The body keeps protesting because it's been trying to protect you for so long. It doesn't know how to stop. But it can learn. And you can help it learn. Not by force. Not by shame. But by showing up, over and over, with patience and presence. That's the work. That's the path. And you're already on it.
